w:r.w:t: The purpose of the project is to provide hands-on training to the students on how to develop a software application from scratch
an action/capability that is expected of a software/product to fulfill
a set of logical actions that need to be performed to fulfill a part of a requirement
Customers can add products to their cart
Customers can track their delivery status
For requirement (1) in the examples, the features that need to be implemented are:
The system should be able to display products to customers
The system should be able to book products and mark them as added to the cart of a customer if the product is in stock
For requirement (2) in the examples, the features that need to be implemented are:
The system should be able to show all undelivered orders from the customer
The system should be able to store data regarding the status of the order
The system should be able to store data regarding the products in the order
The system should be able to show the status of the products and the order to the customer
The language and the environment which is used to develop and run an application
Stack (Linux OS, Apache web server, MySQL database, PHP language)
Stack (MongoDB database, Express JS backend, React JS frontend, Node JS environment)
Students may develop either a web application, an Android application, an iOS application, or a desktop application.
Students may use any tech stack/programming language/scripting to develop their application.
Students may use existing frameworks (Laravel, Express, Angular, Vue) to help with the development as long as they implement the MVC architecture.
(Django, FastAPI and Flask are not permitted)
Students may use libraries/packages to help with the development of their project as long as it

implement any major feature of the project. A library/package implementing any major feature is considered illegal, and the student using the library will be penalized. Consulting the respective faculty member before using a library/package is recommended.

Students must develop the project individually and from scratch. They may not take help from any source, copy code from any source, or use any tutorials that directly show how to implement the project that they have chosen.
Students may not use a code base that they/others may have worked on before.
Students must select a project that they want to implement, which is of lower to moderate complexity.
Students must define the requirements and the features of their project while submitting their project idea.
, and each student is individually responsible for completing the project as a team.
to complete the entire software implementation.
The sprint duration will be approximately
submit their project idea, requirements, and feature before the beginning of the first sprint.

A section faculty may change the project, the requirements, and/or the features if the section faculty determines that the project is not complex enough, or too complex, or for purposes of removing potential duplication.
Students need to submit their work progress through a
at the end of each sprint.
as a version control tool for their project and add the section faculty as a collaborator to the repository during the first sprint.
Each student must push their code to the project repo at the end of each sprint, regardless of whether there are incomplete features, errors, or bugs.
Students must complete their projects within four (4) sprints.
After the sprints, each student must attend a 10-minute viva where
They will show their project demo in 5 minutes.
They will be asked questions about their project code individually for 5 minutes.
All vivas will be recorded and stored for review purposes, if possible.
The viva may be taken offline or online, and the dates and times will be assigned by the section faculty.
Software Requirement Specification (SRS) Document and version control (git)
Implementation of MVC architecture and Project quality
Acceptable report, but lacks clarity and structure
Well-structured and mostly complete, minor gaps
Exceptionally detailed, clear, and organized SRS following all guidelines
No Git activity and sprint submission
Very few commits and sprint submissions
Frequent commits with proper branches and teamwork
Excellent use of Git: commits, issues, branches, and collaboration clearly shown
Project completion (frontend and backend) and quality (unique idea)
Functional but lacks polish or innovation
Creative, polished, fully working frontend and backend with good design
MVC applied, but missing full separation
Proper MVC with separation and reusable code
Clean, modular, maintainable MVC with excellent adherence to design principles
No contribution or unable to answer questions
Moderate understanding with decent explanation of own tasks
Clear explanation, confident, used tools effectively
Deep understanding of the entire project, clear logic, confident use of tech and architecture
